projects.title: Understanding Pandemic Spread and Optimizing Resource Allocation
projects.subtitle: Spatial epidemiology, outbreak forecasting, and intervention design across human, veterinary, and agricultural settings.
projects.summary: Mathematical models and simulations to predict infectious disease spread and guide resource allocation.
projects.description: This project serves as the lab's umbrella effort for spatially explicit epidemic modeling, public-health decision support, and resource allocation under uncertainty. The work combines extended SIR and SEIR families, room-level and regional simulations, policy optimization, and reinforcement-learning-based control to study airborne outbreaks, wartime disruption, agricultural pandemics, and dual-use healthcare systems. Where data are sparse, we use calibrated mechanistic models and scenario analysis to produce actionable recommendations rather than black-box forecasts alone.
outcomes: Decision-support models for outbreak control under mobility, warfare, and infrastructure constraints
Resource-allocation frameworks for dual-use hospital and community health systems
Cross-domain epidemic analyses spanning airborne, zoonotic, and agricultural pathogens

timeline.title: Current umbrella launched
timeline.text: The lab consolidated several ongoing epidemiological lines into a shared project focused on forecasting, intervention design, and allocation.

timeline.title: Dual-use and resilience studies
timeline.text: Work expanded toward wartime healthcare administration and pandemic-aware supply-chain resilience.

timeline.title: Wildlife and field epidemiology
timeline.text: New calibrated models addressed rabies outbreaks in jackals and continued the lab's agricultural outbreak modeling track.

projects.title: Biomathematical Modeling of Cancer and Beyond
projects.subtitle: Multi-scale tumor, immune, and therapy models built from ODEs, PDEs, and agent-based simulations.
projects.summary: Developing bio-mathematical models for cancer spread and treatment, and for additional diseases, using ODEs, PDEs, and simulations.
projects.description: This project develops mechanistic models of disease progression with a current emphasis on cancer growth, treatment scheduling, and treatment-induced senescence. The lab combines continuum models, spatial PDE systems, and agent-based simulations to reason across scales, from cellular interactions to tumor-level response. The goal is not only to fit outcomes, but to expose biological mechanisms, compare treatment schedules, and evaluate hypotheses that are difficult to test experimentally.
outcomes: Therapy-combination models for lung and metastatic prostate cancer
Bridges between PDE and agent-based representations of tumor geometry
Mechanistic tools for evaluating senolytic and immunotherapy strategies

timeline.title: Project formalized
timeline.text: The cancer-modeling line was organized into a focused program around therapy optimization and multi-scale disease dynamics.

timeline.title: Therapy-combination papers published
timeline.text: The lab published models for senolytic combinations in lung cancer and metastatic prostate cancer.

timeline.title: Model comparison and expansion
timeline.text: The project expanded toward comparing PDE and agent-based representations and extending the framework to additional diseases.

projects.title: Data-Driven Precise and Personalized Medicine
projects.subtitle: Clinical prediction, treatment recommendation, and operational decision support built from real healthcare data.
projects.summary: Harnessing AI to tailor treatments to individuals and improve medical resource allocation at the community level.
projects.description: This project connects several of the lab's clinical AI efforts under a single precision-medicine umbrella. The work spans perioperative prediction, clinical text understanding, hospital operations, lung-cancer risk modeling, and decision support for emergency departments. Models are designed to be useful in practice: interpretable when possible, explainable when necessary, and aware of uncertainty when decisions affect patient outcomes or limited medical resources.
outcomes: Risk-stratification tools for perioperative and oncology settings
Clinical decision-support models for surgery and emergency medicine
Resource-allocation frameworks for hospitals and community healthcare systems

timeline.title: Precision-medicine line consolidated
timeline.text: Clinical data pipelines and shared problem definitions were established across perioperative care, oncology, and decision support.

timeline.title: Operational and clinical prototypes
timeline.text: Hospital-allocation and perioperative prediction models matured into a reusable decision-support stack.

timeline.title: Validation and expansion
timeline.text: The project broadened toward richer clinical validation, more explainable recommendations, and prospective collaborations.

projects.title: Developing the Next Step of Physics-Inspired Symbolic Regression
projects.subtitle: Interpretable equation discovery that blends domain knowledge, modern machine learning, and scientific priors.
projects.summary: Advancing physics-informed symbolic regression for interpretable models discovered from data.
projects.description: This project advances symbolic regression from a niche equation-search tool into a practical scientific machine-learning pipeline. The lab studies how to inject domain knowledge, distill strong black-box predictors into symbolic forms, and improve out-of-distribution robustness without sacrificing interpretability. The resulting systems are aimed at physics, engineering, and structured-data settings where researchers need both predictive performance and equations they can inspect.
outcomes: Open frameworks for knowledge-aware symbolic regression
Improved accuracy through symbolic feature engineering and distillation
More robust equation recovery under noise, sparse data, and distribution shift

timeline.title: Program launched
timeline.text: The lab formalized its scientific-ML line around symbolic regression, interpretability, and physically grounded discovery.

timeline.title: Framework and feature-engineering results
timeline.text: Foundational work introduced domain-knowledge integration and symbolic regression as a feature-engineering layer for downstream models.

timeline.title: Distillation and LLM-guided methods
timeline.text: The project expanded to teacher-student distillation and large-language-model-based knowledge injection.

publications.title: Follow the Forest Trail: Distillation by Gradient Boosting Models to Enhance Symbolic Regression Performance

projects.title: Moving Computational Economy To the AI Era
projects.subtitle: Hidden-economy estimation, incentive modeling, and public-policy analysis through machine learning and simulation.
projects.summary: Using AI to uncover patterns, behaviors, and socio-economic impacts in the formal and informal economy.
projects.description: This project examines how AI can help measure hidden economic activity, model institutional incentives, and analyze the behavior of street-level bureaucracies. Rather than treating the informal economy as a single scalar target, the work studies its structure, local variation, and policy sensitivity. The project mixes machine learning, deep learning, and agent-based reasoning to connect economic signals with institutional behavior and public outcomes.
methods: Machine learning on structured economic data
outcomes: Models for estimating the size and local structure of unregistered economic activity
Behavioral analyses of incentives and prosociality in public institutions
Decision-support tools for policy design under institutional and social constraints

timeline.title: Project launched
timeline.text: The lab formalized its hidden-economy and policy-simulation work into a single computational social-science track.

timeline.title: Unregistered-economy estimation
timeline.text: Deep-learning and machine-learning pipelines were used to estimate non-observed economic activity from structured data.

timeline.title: Institutional behavior studies
timeline.text: The project expanded toward incentive design, emergency readiness, and geographic analysis of hidden economic behavior.

projects.title: A Biologically-Informed and Data-Driven Approach to Scientometrics
projects.subtitle: Meta-science models that treat scholarly systems as evolving, competitive, and adaptive populations.
projects.summary: Leveraging biological principles and advanced analytics to evaluate scientific impact and trends.
projects.description: This project studies the behavior of science itself through a computational lens. The lab borrows ideas from ecology, adaptation, and population dynamics to model publication behavior, collaboration structure, research excellence, and writing-style evolution. The aim is to move scientometrics beyond simple counts by building mechanistic and data-driven indicators that better reflect scholarly trajectories and field-level dynamics.
outcomes: New indicators for scholarly influence, publishing strategy, and collaboration behavior
Models of individual researchers' writing-style and venue-selection dynamics
Computational tools for analyzing co-authorship structures and LLM-assisted scientific writing

timeline.title: Program started
timeline.text: The lab launched its meta-science line around scholarly behavior, impact, and field dynamics.

timeline.title: Profiles and writing studies
timeline.text: Early outputs focused on co-authorship panels, writing-style dynamics, and LLM-assisted scientific communication.

timeline.title: Impact and publishing behavior
timeline.text: The project expanded toward home venues, solo authorship, and citation-based excellence indicators.

projects.title: Physics-Informed Diffusion Models for Symbolic Regression
projects.subtitle: Generative data enrichment for recovering equations from sparse, noisy, and partially observed dynamics.
projects.summary: Developing physics-informed temporal diffusion models as a data-enrichment method for symbolic regression.
projects.description: This project focuses on one of the lab's newer scientific-ML directions: using diffusion models to generate physically consistent trajectories that improve symbolic-regression performance. The central idea is to enrich scarce or noisy measurements with synthetic samples that preserve governing dynamics, boundary conditions, or conservation structure. This makes the downstream symbolic model less brittle and more likely to recover compact equations from realistic data.
outcomes: A pipeline for generating physically plausible synthetic trajectories
Benchmarks for measuring symbolic-regression gains from diffusion-based enrichment
A bridge between modern generative models and interpretable scientific discovery

timeline.title: Project kickoff
timeline.text: Initial work defined the data-enrichment problem and the target family of physical systems for evaluation.

timeline.title: Generative backbone in place
timeline.text: The team assembled early diffusion pipelines for temporally structured physical trajectories.

timeline.title: Integration with symbolic regression
timeline.text: Experiments shifted toward measuring whether enriched datasets improve equation recovery under noise and scarcity.

publications.title: Knowledge integration for physics-informed symbolic regression using pre-trained large language models

projects.title: Data-Driven Economic Dynamics with Physics and Reinforcement Learning
projects.subtitle: Best-guess project framing based on the current slug, team descriptions, and the lab's computational-economy work.
projects.summary: Using reinforcement learning and dynamical-systems ideas to study controllable microeconomic processes.
projects.description: This page is filled using the strongest local evidence available: the project slug, the assigned student descriptions, and adjacent publications in the lab's computational-economy portfolio. The current direction appears to focus on modeling microeconomic processes as controllable dynamical systems, borrowing intuition from physics and using reinforcement learning to search for interventions or control policies. The page therefore emphasizes market-like simulation, policy optimization, and transfer of control ideas between physical and economic systems.
outcomes: Prototype environments for controllable microeconomic dynamics
Policy-search methods for intervention and allocation tasks
A shared methodology connecting economic simulation, RL, and physical-system analogies

timeline.title: Concept formation
timeline.text: The project direction emerged around RL-guided control of economic processes using physically inspired abstractions.

timeline.title: Methodological bridge-building
timeline.text: The team aligned the work with the lab's existing economy and control literature to define suitable benchmarks and tasks.

timeline.title: Early benchmark and experimentation phase
timeline.text: Current work focuses on simulation environments, reward design, and the first comparative experiments.

projects.title: Cybersecurity Federated Learning
projects.subtitle: Privacy-preserving collaborative learning for distributed anomaly detection and cyber defense.
projects.summary: Developing and applying federated learning techniques to enhance cybersecurity defenses without sharing sensitive raw data.
projects.description: This project explores how federated learning can be adapted to cybersecurity settings where organizations need collective intelligence without centralizing sensitive logs, telemetry, or device-level data. The current emphasis is on privacy-preserving anomaly detection, distributed threat modeling, communication-efficient training, and robustness to non-iid or even adversarial participants. Compared with the lab's more publication-heavy projects, this line appears newer and more exploratory, so the page focuses on the research agenda, team, and planned outcomes.
outcomes: A benchmark setup for distributed cyber-defense tasks
Prototype federated models for anomaly and threat detection
Evaluation protocols for privacy, robustness, and real-world deployment constraints

timeline.title: Project started
timeline.text: The team began defining the threat models, deployment assumptions, and privacy constraints for federated cybersecurity experiments.

timeline.title: Benchmark construction
timeline.text: Early work centers on identifying datasets, training topologies, and robustness baselines for collaborative cyber-defense.

timeline.title: Prototype evaluation
timeline.text: The next step is to compare centralized, local, and federated approaches under realistic data fragmentation and attack assumptions.

projects.title: Understanding Animals' Behavior and Improving Their Wellness using AI
projects.subtitle: Computer vision, acoustics, and behavioral modeling for animal welfare, health, and social interaction.
projects.summary: Analyzing behavioral and sensorial data to derive insights into animal health and welfare.
projects.description: This project studies animal behavior and welfare through data-driven sensing and computational ethology. The lab uses video, landmarks, vocalizations, kinematics, and physiological signals to detect pain, stress, social dynamics, and environmental effects in companion, captive, and farm animals. Across species, the shared goal is to extract practical welfare indicators from non-invasive observations while also learning more about communication and behavior.
outcomes: Non-invasive indicators for pain, stress, welfare, and communication
Cross-species computational tools for cats, dogs, cattle, chimpanzees, insects, and more
Practical AI pipelines that support veterinarians, shelters, and animal-behavior researchers

timeline.title: Program launched
timeline.text: The lab consolidated its animal-AI work around welfare assessment, facial signaling, and non-invasive sensing.

timeline.title: Computer-vision expansion
timeline.text: Multiple projects targeted cats, dogs, cattle, and insect larvae with visual and multimodal pipelines.

timeline.title: Behavior and social-communication studies
timeline.text: The project deepened into chimpanzee social ranking, cat facial signaling, and broader welfare-oriented analyses.
